questions.question: Why is schema evolution harder in event systems than in tightly coupled request-response APIs?
options: Consumers often upgrade on different schedules and may replay old events long after producers changed.
Events cannot carry structured fields at all.
Event systems never need compatibility rules because they are asynchronous.
Only one consumer can exist for each event type.
questions.explanation: Loose coupling helps fanout, but it also creates many independent upgrade timelines.

questions.question: Which change is usually safest for an existing event schema?
options: Adding an optional field with clear defaults and preserved semantics.
Renaming a field and reusing the old meaning implicitly.
Adding a new required field that all old consumers must read immediately.
Changing the event meaning while keeping the same type name.
questions.explanation: Additive optional changes are the usual safe default for long-lived event contracts.

questions.question: What is the biggest risk of changing event semantics in place without a new version or type?
options: Consumers may continue decoding successfully while interpreting the event incorrectly.
The event bus will always reject the payload.
Replay will become faster than real-time processing.
Storage costs for old events will disappear.
questions.explanation: Semantic drift is dangerous because the schema may still parse while the meaning becomes wrong.

questions.question: Which signal most directly shows a consumer was broken by a producer schema change?
options: A spike in deserialization failures or dead-letter events for that consumer and version.
A decrease in product roadmap size.
Fewer dashboards being viewed by operators.
A drop in HTTP cache hit ratio.
questions.explanation: Consumer decode failures and DLQ growth are strong immediate signals of compatibility breakage.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with keeping deprecated fields around during migrations?
options: It helps slow consumers survive, but increases contract clutter and migration overhead.
It eliminates the need for schema governance.
It guarantees no consumer inventory is needed.
It forces every event to become snapshot-based.
questions.explanation: Backward compatibility often buys safety by keeping extra contract surface alive for longer.

questions.question: When is dual-publish or an upcaster most justified?
options: When semantic incompatibility is real and consumers cannot all switch at once.
When adding a harmless optional field.
When the event bus has spare storage space.
When no consumers exist yet.
questions.explanation: Dual-publish and translation mechanisms are expensive tools best used for real compatibility gaps.

questions.question: Why must replay be part of the schema evolution discussion?
options: New consumers and backfills may need to process old event history under current expectations.
Replay only matters in databases, not event systems.
It removes the need for version tags.
It guarantees exactly-once semantics.
questions.explanation: A design that only works for fresh events is incomplete in many production event systems.

questions.question: What is the strongest default governance mechanism for event schemas?
options: Registry-backed compatibility checks before producers deploy breaking changes.
A shared chat room where teams mention schema changes informally.
Trusting every consumer to notice changes by reading payloads manually.
Allowing any producer to rewrite event history after publish.
questions.explanation: Automated compatibility checks reduce the chance of accidental ecosystem-wide breakage.
